137	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
138	
139	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
140	
141	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. 142	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
143	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.

145	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.

5	\section{The Albanese construction}
6	For a smooth proper curve \(C\) over \(k\), the Albanese variety is the universal abelian variety receiving a morphism from \(C\). Over an algebraically closed field this is classically identified with the connected component of the identity of the Picard scheme, but we construct it directly from symmetric powers.
7	\subsection{Symmetric powers and the Abel--Jacobi map}
8	Let \(C^{(g)} = \Sym^g(C)\) be the \(g\)\textsuperscript{th} symmetric power of \(C\) (the quotient of \(C^g\) by the action of the symmetric group \(S_g\)). It is a smooth proper scheme over \(k\) of dimension \(g\). Fix a \(k\)-rational point \(P_0 \in C(k)\). The map
9	\[
10	  \sigma \colon C^{(g)} \longrightarrow \Pic^g_{C/k}, \qquad
11	  \sum_{i=1}^g [P_i] \longmapsto \mathcal O_C\bigl(\textstyle\sum_{i=1}^g P_i - g P_0\bigr)
12	\]
13	is surjective and birational onto its image; its fibres are projective spaces (Brill--Noether--Riemann--Roch). Choosing \(P_0\) gives a translation isomorphism \(\Pic^g_{C/k} \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} \Pic^0_{C/k}\), and the Stein factorisation of \(\sigma\) yields the Jacobian as the connected component of the identity.
14	In the Lean formalisation we do not have general symmetric powers of schemes (or quotients by finite group actions) in Mathlib. We therefore take a more direct approach: we define the Jacobian as the Albanese object of \(C\) inside the category of smooth proper group schemes over \(k\), using the universal property and the known dimension formula.

92	\subsection{The Jacobian definition}
93	\begin{definition}\leanok
94	[Jacobian]
95	  \uses{def:genus, def:IsAlbanese, def:JacobianWitness, thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}
96	  \label{def:Jacobian}
97	  
98	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian}
99	  The \emph{Jacobian} of \(C\) is the abelian variety \(\Jac(C)\) over \(k\) obtained as the Albanese object of \(C\): a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme over \(k\) of dimension \(g(C)\) equipped with a universal morphism \(C \to \Jac(C)\) (for each choice of marked point). It is viewed as an object of the category of \(k\)-schemes over \(\Spec k\). Formally, \(\Jac(C)\) is defined as the underlying scheme of a (uniform-over-\(P\)) Albanese witness for \(C\) provided by Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}.
100	  When \(g(C) = 0\) the Jacobian collapses to \(\Spec k\) itself, which is the terminal object in the category of \(k\)-schemes. When \(g(C) > 0\) the underlying scheme is the abelian variety produced by Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}; the genus-one case recovers \(C\) itself (after choosing a \(k\)-rational point), and the higher genus case yields the non-trivial \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety. Both cases are handled uniformly: \(\Jac(C)\) is the underlying scheme of the Albanese witness, with no special-case branching at the level of the definition. The genus-\(0\) specialisation is implicit in the fact that any group scheme over \(k\) of relative dimension \(0\) is isomorphic to \(\Spec k\).
101	\end{definition}
102	\section{Group scheme structure and abelian-variety properties}
103	The four protected instances on \(\Jac(C)\) are each obtained, at the level of the Lean formalisation, by projecting a field of the underlying Albanese witness produced by Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}. The mathematical content of each property is the corresponding statement about the Albanese variety of a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve.

190	\subsection{Sanity check in low genus}
191	\begin{itemize}
192	  \item \(g = 0\): Then \(\Jac(C) = \Spec k\), the trivial group scheme; \(\Jac(\mathbb P^1_k) = \Spec k\) is smooth of relative dimension \(0 = g\).
193	  \item \(g = 1\): Then \(\Jac(C) \cong C\) once a \(k\)-rational point on \(C\) is fixed; \(C\) is itself an elliptic curve (abelian variety of dimension \(1\)).
194	  \item \(g \ge 2\): \(\Jac(C)\) is a non-trivial \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety, never isomorphic to \(C\) as a scheme.
195	\end{itemize}
196	\section{Existence of an Albanese variety}
197	The classical construction of the Jacobian goes through the Picard scheme machinery: \(\Jac(C)\) is the connected component of the identity of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\). An equivalent route, available whenever a \(k\)-rational point \(P \in C(k)\) has been fixed, is to take the Stein factorisation of the Abel--Jacobi morphism \(\Sym^g(C) \to \Pic^g_{C/k}\) and translate by \(-gP\).
198	\subsection{The Albanese witness bundle}
199	Before stating the existence theorem, we package its conclusion into a single structure --- the \emph{Albanese witness} of \(C\). This bundling reverses the quantifier order of the classical statement (see Remark~\ref{rem:JacobianWitness_quantifier_order}) and is the data structure consumed by the \texttt{Jacobian} family of declarations in this file and by the \texttt{AbelJacobi} interface of Chapter~\ref{chap:AbelJacobi}.

210	    \item \(\mathtt{grpObj}\): a group-object structure on \(J\) in the category of \(k\)-schemes over \(\Spec k\).
211	    \item \(\mathtt{proper}\): a proof that the structure morphism \(J \to \Spec k\) is proper.
212	    \item \(\mathtt{smooth}\): a proof that the structure morphism \(J \to \Spec k\) is smooth.
213	    \item \(\mathtt{geomIrred}\): a proof that the structure morphism \(J \to \Spec k\) is geometrically irreducible.
214	    \item \(\mathtt{smoothGenus}\): a proof that the structure morphism \(J \to \Spec k\) is smooth of relative dimension \(g(C)\). This refines \(\mathtt{smooth}\) at the specific relative dimension \(\genus\, C\) (see Remark~\ref{rem:JacobianWitness_smooth_redundancy}).

258	\begin{proof}
259	  The proof is split into three classical constructions plus the genus-\(0\) sub-case absorbed into the same witness (the latter packaged as the explicit construction \cref{def:genusZeroWitness}, which routes through the characteristic-free keystone \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} of \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity} --- rigidity proved over the algebraic closure \(\bar k = \mathrm{AlgebraicClosure}\,k\) --- and then \emph{descends} the resulting morphism equality from \(\bar k\) to the base field \(k\) along the faithfully-flat surjection \(\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k\)). Each route produces the same intrinsic scheme \(J\); only the universal morphism \(\iota_P \colon C \to J\) varies with the marked point \(P\). We outline each route and name the Mathlib infrastructure each one requires.
260	  \paragraph{(0) Reduction to a \(P\)-independent witness.}
261	  The witness \(J\) depends only on \(C\), not on the marked point \(P\). Indeed, both classical constructions below produce \(J\) as an intrinsic object of \(C\) (the identity component of \(\Pic_{C/k}\), or the Stein-factorisation target of \(\Sym^g(C) \to \Pic^g_{C/k}\) translated by a fixed \(P_0\)); a different choice of \(P\) in \(C(k)\) alters \(\iota_P\) by a translation of \(J\), which is an automorphism, and so does not change \(J\) as a scheme. The Lean encoding reflects this by bundling \(J\) as a single field and the universal-property data as \(\forall P,\, \mathrm{IsAlbanese}\, C\, P\, J\). We now turn to producing \(J\).
262	  \paragraph{Route A --- Picard scheme.}
263	  This route follows Hartshorne~III.4 and FGA Explained, Chapter~9.
264	  \begin{itemize}
265	    \item[\textbf{(A.1)}] \emph{The relative Picard functor.} For a \(k\)-scheme \(T\), write \(C_T := C \times_k T\) and let \(\pi \colon C_T \to T\) be the projection. The relative Picard functor is
266	    \[
267	      \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k} \colon (\Sch/k)^{\mathrm{op}} \to \Sets, \qquad
268	      T \longmapsto \Pic(C_T) / \pi^*\Pic(T).
269	    \]
270	    A \(T\)-point is a line bundle on \(C_T\) modulo a line bundle pulled back from \(T\). The set-valued formulation suffices for representability; the canonical abelian-group structure on \(\Pic(C_T)\) descends to the quotient by \(\pi^*\Pic(T)\) and gives \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}\) a natural group-functor refinement.
271	    \item[\textbf{(A.2)}] \emph{Representability.} For \(C\) smooth proper geometrically connected over \(k\) with a \(k\)-rational point, \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}\) (after \'etale sheafification, if \(k\) is not algebraically closed) is representable by a \(k\)-group scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\), locally of finite type. This is FGA's main representability theorem (Grothendieck, FGA~232); the smooth-proper-geometrically-connected hypothesis ensures that the cohomology-and-base-change machinery applies and that \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) = k\), which is needed for the quotient by \(\pi^*\Pic(T)\) to be \'etale-locally injective.

The \emph{Rigidity Lemma} (\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}, Mumford Form~I) is an elementary properness/closed-map argument --- cohomology-free --- and supplies the collapse of a complete-factor map. From it, the \emph{additive decomposition} Corollary~1.5 (\cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}) and \emph{Corollary~1.2} that a pointed regular map of abelian varieties is a homomorphism follow directly. The single-curve base case --- that every morphism \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A\) is constant --- is supplied \emph{not} by the cube but by the \(\mathbb G_a/\mathbb G_m\) group-law incompatibility (Milne Proposition~3.9, \cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial}): the additive-defect map \(\psi(x,y) = f(x+y) - f(x) - f(y)\), defined a priori only on the affine surface \(\mathbb G_a \times \mathbb G_a\), is extended to the complete surface \(\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1\) by the rational-map-extension Theorem~3.2/3.4 (\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}) and then collapsed by the Rigidity Lemma, forcing \(f|_{\mathbb G_a}\) to be an additive homomorphism; the parallel relation \(f(xy) = f(x) + f(y) + c\) on \(\mathbb G_m\) is incompatible with it unless \(f\) is constant. 487	\begin{proof}
488	  We construct the seven fields of \cref{def:JacobianWitness}.
489	  \paragraph{Underlying scheme.} Take \(J := \mathbf{1}\), the terminal object of \(\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)\), which on underlying schemes is \(\Spec k\) viewed as a \(k\)-scheme via the identity structure morphism.
490	  \paragraph{Group-object structure, properness, smoothness, geometric irreducibility.} The terminal object \(\mathbf{1}\) of any cartesian-monoidal category carries the (unique) trivial group-object structure: multiplication and inverse are the unique morphisms to \(\mathbf{1}\), and the identity element is the identity morphism. The structure morphism \(\mathbf{1}.\mathtt{hom} = \mathrm{id}_{\Spec k}\) is proper (the identity is a closed immersion, in particular proper), smooth (the identity is unramified and flat, hence smooth), and geometrically irreducible. The geometric-irreducibility instance is the project-local helper \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.geometricallyIrreducible\_id\_Spec} (\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Jacobian.lean:134}--\texttt{140}), which derives geometric irreducibility of the identity on \(\Spec k\) from the fact that, on the geometric fibre \(\Spec K\) over any field extension \(k \to K\), the second projection of the pullback square is an isomorphism, and \(\Spec K\) is irreducible.
491	  \paragraph{Smoothness of relative dimension \(\genus\,C\).} The smoothness witness \(\mathtt{smoothGenus}\) is required at relative dimension \(\genus\,C\). The hypothesis \(\genus\,C = 0\) rewrites the target relative dimension to \(0\), after which the goal reduces to \(\mathtt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\ 0\ (\mathrm{id}_{\Spec k})\). This is a Mathlib instance: the identity morphism on any base is smooth of relative dimension \(0\).
492	  \paragraph{The \texttt{isAlbaneseFor} field.} For every \(k\)-rational marked point \(P : \mathbf{1} \to C\), we exhibit an \cref{def:IsAlbanese} term for the triple \((C, P, J)\).
493	  The universal pointed morphism is the unique terminal map \(\alpha := \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \colon C \to \mathbf{1}\) (the structure morphism of \(C\) as a \(k\)-scheme, factored through the terminal object). The pointed condition \(P \circ \alpha = \eta_J = \mathrm{id}_{\mathbf{1}}\) holds automatically: both sides are morphisms \(\mathbf{1} \to \mathbf{1}\) in \(\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)\), and the terminal object has a unique endomorphism.
494	  For the universal factorisation: given a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme \(A\) over \(k\) with identity element \(\eta_A \colon \mathbf{1} \to A\) and a morphism \(f \colon C \to A\) with \(P \circ f = \eta_A\), we must exhibit a unique morphism \(g \colon J \to A\) such that \(f = \alpha \circ g\). \emph{Existence}: take \(g := \eta_A \colon \mathbf{1} \to A\). Then \(\alpha \circ g = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \circ \eta_A\), which equals \(f\) precisely when \(f\) is the constant morphism at \(\eta_A\). We obtain this equality by descent from the algebraic closure \(\bar k = \mathrm{AlgebraicClosure}\,k\). Base-change the data along \(\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k\): the curve \(C_{\bar k}\) is again smooth proper geometrically irreducible of genus \(0\) (genus is stable under base change for a geometrically irreducible curve), the group scheme \(A_{\bar k}\) is again smooth proper geometrically irreducible, the base-changed point \(P_{\bar k}\) is a \(\bar k\)-rational point, and \(f_{\bar k}\) still kills it: \(f_{\bar k}(P_{\bar k}) = \eta_{A_{\bar k}}\). By \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} (the project's characteristic-free genus-\(0\) rigidity keystone of \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity}, carrying \([\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]\) and \emph{no} \([\mathtt{CharZero}]\) hypothesis; it lives in the upstream \texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean}, imported by \texttt{Jacobian.lean}, so consuming it here introduces no import cycle), \(f_{\bar k}\) equals the constant morphism \(\mathtt{toUnit}\,C_{\bar k} \circ \eta_{A_{\bar k}}\) over \(\bar k\). Finally we descend this morphism equality back to \(k\): the projection \(p \colon C_{\bar k} \to C\) is the base change of the faithfully-flat surjection \(\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k\), hence faithfully flat and surjective, hence an epimorphism of schemes by \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Flat.epi\_of\_flat\_of\_surjective}; right-cancelling \(p\) against the base-change-square identities yields \(f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \circ \eta_A\) as \(k\)-scheme morphisms (Sub-step~C.2.f of \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}). \emph{Uniqueness}: if \(g_1, g_2 \colon \mathbf{1} \to A\) both satisfy \(f = \alpha \circ g_i\) with \(\alpha = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C\), then \(\alpha \circ g_1 = \alpha \circ g_2\), and we right-cancel \(\alpha\). This is the \emph{same} epi-cancellation as in the Existence paragraph: the underlying scheme morphism of \(\alpha\) is \(C.\mathtt{hom} \colon C \to \Spec k\) (\texttt{Over.toUnit\_left}), which is faithfully flat and surjective (a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve is faithfully flat and surjective over its base field), hence an epimorphism of schemes by \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Flat.epi\_of\_flat\_of\_surjective}; therefore \(\alpha\) is an epimorphism in \(\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)\) by \texttt{Over.epi\_of\_epi\_left}, and \texttt{cancel\_epi} gives \(g_1 = g_2\). Uniqueness does \emph{not} follow from terminality of \(\mathbf{1}\): morphisms \emph{out} of a terminal object are global points and are not unique in general (terminality gives uniqueness only of morphisms \emph{into} \(\mathbf{1}\)).
495	  \paragraph{Vacuity for the \(C(k) = \emptyset\) branch.} When no \(k\)-rational point \(P : \mathbf{1} \to C\) exists (Brauer--Severi conics over \(\mathbb Q\) are the standard counterexample), the universal quantifier \(\forall (P : \mathbf{1} \to C), \mathtt{IsAlbanese}\,C\,P\,J\) is vacuously satisfied at the Lean type level: Lean's \(\forall\) over an empty type is trivially true.
